findings.message: The route now imports from `..db.item_service`, a SECOND data-access module that owns `Item` persistence in parallel to the existing `..db.repository` (already imported on the next line). Both modules expose overlapping Item read/write helpers (item_service.add_item/fetch_all_items vs repository.list_items), so this route is coupled to two duplicate data-access layers for the same model. The module's own docstring calls this a 'duplicate-data-access-layer smell'. Routes should depend on a single persistence boundary.
findings.suggestion: Pick one data-access module for Item. Add a `create_item`/insert helper to `repository.py` and import it there, then delete `item_service.py` (or fold its unique behavior into repository). Drop the `..db.item_service` import from the route.

findings.message: `fetch_all_items` is imported but never used in `create_item` (or anywhere in the diff), adding an unnecessary dependency edge from the route to the duplicate `item_service` module.
findings.suggestion: Remove the unused `fetch_all_items` import; if unpaginated full-table reads are ever needed, expose them through the single chosen repository module.

findings.message: `create_item` drives the transaction boundary directly in the HTTP route (`session.commit()` + `session.refresh()`), because `item_service.add_item` deliberately flushes-but-does-not-commit. This pushes persistence/transaction control up into the route layer rather than keeping it behind the data-access boundary, an inconsistent layering split versus the read path which delegates fully to `repository.list_items`.
findings.suggestion: Let the data-access layer own the commit (e.g. a `repository.create_item` that adds+commits+refreshes and returns the persisted Item), so the route stays a thin HTTP adapter with no direct transaction management.
